description: Canonical few-shot examples for the bulk SMC section summarizer. Each entry covers a distinct archetype (definitions, long substantive policy, penalty/enforcement, permit-procedural, LUC use restrictions) so Sonnet learns the voice and shape across the buckets it'll encounter at scale. Generated with Opus 4.7 against the SECTION_SYSTEM_PROMPT in seattle_app/services/claude_service.py; curated and locked 2026-04-29. To regenerate: `python manage.py bootstrap_section_summaries --sections <numbers>` and replace the matching entry below.

examples.title: Definitions
examples.input_excerpt:
For purposes of this Chapter 8.37:
"Acceptance"
means the initial communication
from an app-based worker to a network company
that the app-based worker intends to perform
services in furtherance of an offer, including but
not limited to indicating acceptance through the
worker platform.
"Adverse action"
means reducing compensation; garnishing tips or gratuities; temporarily or
permanently denying or limiting access to work,
incentives, or bonuses; offering less desirable work;
terminating; deactivating; threatening; penalizing; retaliating; engaging in unfair immigrationrelated practices; filing a false report with a government agency; or otherwise discriminating
against any person for any reason prohibited by
"Adverse action"
Section 8.37.120. for an appbased worker may involve any aspect of the appbased worker's work, including compensation,
work hours, volume, and frequency of offers made
available, desirability and compensation rates of
offers made available, responsibilities, or o
examples.summary:
This section is administrative. It is the dictionary for Seattle's chapter on app-based workers (sometimes called gig workers), defining the words used throughout the rest of the chapter. It does not create rules on its own; instead, it tells you how to read the rules in other sections. Below is a map of what is defined, grouped by topic, so you can find a term when you encounter it elsewhere.

People and businesses involved: app-based worker, network company, on-demand network company, marketplace network company, customer, paying customer, aggrieved party, respondent, franchise.

How work is offered and arranged: offer, on-demand offer, pre-scheduled offer, online order, acceptance, application dispatch, worker platform, cancellation with cause, cancellation without cause.

Where and when work happens: operating in Seattle, perform services in Seattle, engaged time, engaged miles.

Pay, costs, and rate-setting: compensation, network company payment, incentive, tips, minimum wage equivalent rate, standard mileage rate, associated cost factor, associated time factor, associated mileage factor, rate of inflation, front pay.

Worker protections language: adverse action.

Types of establishments and services that come up in the rules: eating and drinking establishment, food processing, creative works or services, service subject to professional licensure, unsealed (items).

Agencies, officials, and procedure: City, Agency (Office of Labor Standards), Director, Hearing Examiner, written / in writing.

A few practical notes built into these definitions: the chapter does not cover transportation network companies (like rideshare), taxis, or for-hire vehicles, which are regulated elsewhere; it also excludes certain transactions such as product sales, fully digital services, and creative work. The Director of the Office of Labor Standards has authority to issue rules clarifying several definitions, including engaged time, online orders, and which professional licenses are on the excluded list. If you run into a defined term while reading another part of Chapter 8.37, come back here for its precise meaning.

examples.title: Violations and Penalties
examples.input_excerpt:
A. Violations. It is a violation of this code to:
1. Violate or fail to comply with any
requirement of this code;
2. Violate or fail to comply with a final
order, a stop work order, an emergency order or a
hazard correction order issued pursuant to the
provisions of this code;
3. Act in a manner prohibited by this
code or a permit, approval, rule, or order issued
pursuant to this code;
4. Aid, abet, counsel, encourage, incite,
induce, hire or otherwise procure another person
to violate this code;
5. Remove, mutilate, destroy or conceal
any notice or order issued or posted by the Director pursuant to the provisions of this code, or any
notice or order issued or posted by the Director in
response to a natural disaster or other emergency; or
6. Make or submit any false or misleading statement or information as part of or in
connection with any application for any grading
permit or any approval under this code.
B. Notice of Violation.
1. Issuance of Notice. If after investigation the Direc
examples.summary:
This section explains how the City enforces its grading code and what happens if you break the rules.

You violate the code if you ignore any of its requirements, disobey a final order, stop-work order, emergency order, or hazard correction order, do something the code or your permit prohibits, help or hire someone else to violate the code, remove or destroy a posted notice from the Director, or submit false information on a grading permit application.

If the Director finds a violation, you may receive a notice of violation by personal delivery, first-class mail, or posting on the property. The notice will say what you did wrong, what you need to fix, and by when. If you want to challenge it, you must submit a written request for review within ten days of service or by the compliance date, whichever comes first. A representative of the Director will review the notice (and any extra information you provide) and may sustain, withdraw, amend, or continue it. The decision comes in writing within 15 days. If you do not request review in time, the notice becomes a final order.

You can be fined up to $500 per day for each ongoing violation. The City enforces these penalties by suing in Seattle Municipal Court, where it must prove the violation by a preponderance of the evidence. Because of this, you cannot challenge the Director's orders through the usual land-use appeal process under state law (Chapter 36.70C RCW). As an alternative, the City may pursue criminal charges, with fines up to $5,000, jail up to 365 days, or both, with each day counted as a separate offense. The City Attorney can also seek court orders to stop the activity or fix the problem, and the Director can record notices against your property in King County records at your expense.

If you are doing unsafe or unpermitted grading work, the Director can issue a stop work order by posting it on site or serving it by mail. You then have two business days to request review, which happens quickly and results in a written decision.

Finally, the City has no legal duty to investigate or enforce, and this chapter does not give private parties the right to sue.

examples.title: Permitted and prohibited uses
examples.input_excerpt:
A. All uses are permitted outright, prohibited or
permitted as a conditional use, according to Table A for
23.50.012 and this Section 23.50.012.
B. All permitted uses are allowed as either a principal use or an accessory use, unless otherwise indicated in Table A for 23.50.012.
C. Public Facilities.
1. Similar Uses Permitted. Except as provided in subsections 23.50.012.C.2 and 23.50.012.C.3
and in Section 23.50.027, uses in public facilities that
are most similar to uses permitted outright or permitted by conditional use in this chapter are also permitted outright or by conditional use, subject to the same
use regulations, development standards and administrative conditional use criteria that govern the similar
uses.
2. Waivers or Modification by City Council
for Similar Uses. The City Council may waive or modify applicable development standards or conditional
use criteria for those uses in public facilities that are
similar to uses permitted outright or permitted by
conditional use ac
examples.summary:
This section sets the rules for what kinds of activities and businesses are allowed in Seattle's industrial zones (IB, IG1, and IG2, including the Duwamish Manufacturing/Industrial Center). The accompanying Table A lists each use—agriculture, manufacturing, offices, schools, parking, utilities, and so on—and marks it as permitted, prohibited, or allowed only with a conditional use permit in each zone. To find out whether a specific use is allowed on your property, you check the table for your zone.

Unless the table says otherwise, any permitted use can be either the main use of the property or an accessory to another use.

For public facilities (things like fire stations, libraries, or transit bases), the section allows uses that are similar to other permitted uses to go through under the same rules. The City Council can waive or modify standards for public facilities through a formal land use decision process, and can also approve unusual public uses that aren't otherwise listed. In industrial zones specifically, the Council can approve a public facility that doesn't meet normal development standards if it provides a unique public service (like police or fire), needs that particular location, needs the waiver to deliver the service, and has been designed to fit the surroundings. Major expansions of public facilities (those exceeding 750 square feet or 10 percent of existing area, or that don't meet standards) go through the same Council process; smaller expansions can use a simpler permit if standards are met.

In the IG1 and IG2 zones, you can place recreational space on the rooftop of a building that existed by the end of 1998, but only as an amenity for office uses of at least 100,000 square feet in the same building. Any rooftop structure covering that space must be one story, no taller than 30 feet or the rest of the building, cover no more than 30 percent of the roof, and use at least 30 percent transparent or translucent materials.

If you want to open or expand an adult cabaret, the property must be at least 800 feet from any community center, child care center, school, or public park, and at least 600 feet from any other adult cabaret. Distances are measured based on conditions when your permit application is complete or when the City's decision is published.

examples.title: Notice of application
examples.input_excerpt:
A. Notice.
1. No notice of application is required for
Type I decisions, except that notice of application is
required for:
a. All projects in MPC zones that are
subject to Master Planned Community design review
in Section 23.41.020, as described in subsection
23.76.012.B.6; and
b. An application for a Type I permit
with an interim design review exemption as described
in subsection 23.41.004.E.3.
2. Within 14 days after the Director determines that an application is complete, for the following
types of applications, the Director shall provide notice
of the application and an opportunity for public comment as described in this Section 23.76.012:
a. An application for Type I permit with
an interim design review exemption as described in
subsection 23.41.004.E.3;
b. Type II Master Use Permits;
c. Type III Master Use Permits;
d. Type IV Council land use decisions,
provided that for amendments to property use and
development agreements, additional notice shall be
given pursuant to subsection
examples.summary:
This section explains how the City tells the public that someone has applied for a land use permit, and how you can comment on it.

When notice is required: Most routine Type I permits get no public notice. But notice is required for Type II and Type III Master Use Permits, certain Type IV and Type V Council land use decisions (like Major Institution designations or City facility approvals), projects in Master Planned Community zones, and Type I permits with an interim design review exemption. The Director must send out notice within 14 days after deciding the application is complete, and must also alert any other government agencies that may have a role.

How you find out: Depending on the project, the City uses some combination of a large notice sign on the property, smaller land use signs at street frontages, ten placards within 300 feet (when a sign isn't practical or for highrise projects in MPC zones), an entry in the Land Use Information Bulletin (the City's official notice), and mailed notices. Mailed notice goes out for things like variances, conditional uses, subdivisions, design review meetings, Major Institution decisions, and similar matters, and to people who attended early design guidance meetings or wrote in about the project. The Director also notifies other government agencies that may have jurisdiction, and for early-review environmental determinations, notifies the State Department of Ecology, affected tribes, and affected local agencies.

The notice in the Bulletin must include details such as the application date, a description of the project, what permits are involved, where to review the file, the comment period and your right to comment, hearing information if any, and appeal rights. Other notices (signs and mailings) include a shorter version of this information.

Commenting: You generally have 14 days to submit written comments to the Director, and you can request a 14-day extension if you do so within the original period. Shoreline applications get a 30-day comment period (20 days for limited utility extensions and bulkheads). Comments are due by 5 p.m. on the last day, with weekends and holidays rolling to the next business day. Late comments may still be considered if relevant to ongoing review. If a permit covers multiple decisions, the broadest notice rules apply. The mailing list for the Bulletin is updated yearly.

examples.title: Specific environmental policies

affected primarily by vehicular emissions which
create (cid:0) hot spots (cid:0) and nonattainment areas (such

plants, and wood stoves, the burning of toxics or
wastes, and other emissions, including odor impacts.

the state inspection/maintenance program, and
public transportation improvements are the primary means of mitigating air quality impacts
from motor vehicles.
e. The Puget Sound Clean Air
Agency is responsible for monitoring air quality
in the Seattle area, setting standards and regulating development to achieve regional air quality goals.

examples.summary:
This section sets out the specific environmental policies that City decisionmakers use under SEPA (the State Environmental Policy Act) to review development projects. For each topic, it explains why the issue matters, notes that other regulations may already cover it, and lists examples of conditions the City can impose if those other rules don't fully address a project's impacts. Decisionmakers can condition or even deny a project to reduce harm, generally subject to an "overview policy" in Section 25.05.665.

The topics covered are air quality, construction impacts (noise, drainage, pedestrian and traffic disruption, mud and dust), drainage and stormwater, earth movement (landslides, erosion, seismic risk), energy use and conservation, environmental health (toxic and hazardous materials), height, bulk and scale of buildings, historic preservation, housing (including demolition of low-rent units and affordable housing impacts of large commercial projects), land use compatibility, light and glare, noise, plants and animals, public services and facilities (sewers, schools, parks, transit, fire and police), public view protection, shadows on parks and other open spaces, traffic and transportation, and water quality.

If you are proposing a project, expect that the City may require studies of likely impacts in any of these areas and may impose conditions such as limits on construction hours, dust and erosion controls, drainage improvements, redesign to reduce height or bulk, façade or landscaping changes, lighting restrictions, transit subsidies, bicycle parking, or street improvements. For commercial buildings over 4,000 square feet in certain zones, you may have to build affordable housing or pay into an affordable housing fund. Projects near designated landmarks may need sympathetic design, and projects on archaeologically sensitive sites can be delayed up to 90 days (sometimes longer) for artifact recovery.

Some protections apply only in listed places. Public views of Mount Rainier, the Olympics, Cascades, downtown skyline, and major waterways are protected from specific viewpoints, parks, and scenic routes listed in Attachment 1. Views of the Space Needle are protected from ten named parks. Shadow protections apply to public parks, schoolyards, shoreline street ends, and five named downtown parks plus Kobe Terrace.
